exemplar.title: Using Linear Regression to determine the outcome of the US Election

detailedExemplarData.title: Using Linear Regression to determine the outcome of the US Election

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear and descriptive title that establishes the focus of the exploration
Identifiable introduction, body and conclusion allow the reader to follow the main idea
Headings (Introduction, Methodology, Data, Evaluation, Bibliography) provide a recognisable structure
weaknesses: Frequent digressions (personal anecdotes, teacherâs notes) interrupt the flow
Corrupt table of contents and misnumbered figures reduce coherence
Missing and misplaced sections (blank Aims, methodology placeholders, tables separated from discussion)
Inconsistent formatting of headings and bibliography entries
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration shows some coherence and a basic structure (band 2). The reader can follow the main idea and recognises sections, but digressions, teacher annotations, corrupt contents and misplacement of tables lower overall organization and coherence to band 2 rather than higher levels.

strengths: Correct use of terms such as âcorrelation coefficient,â âr valueâ and âline of best fitâ
Inclusion of multiple representations (tables, scatter plots) with thoughtful captions
Plotting the mean as central point shows analytical insight
weaknesses: Variables are not explicitly defined and calculator-specific syntax (e.g., âL1 Barâ) appears
Axes and units are unlabelled or hand-drawn, and regression equations are omitted
Table headers are meaningless (e.g., âABC 123 123â) and reduce clarity
Logical links (e.g., why weak r invalidates prediction) are missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The communication demonstrates some appropriate mathematical language, notation and representation (band 2). Tables and graphs are present but lack proper labels and regression details, and key logical links are only partially communicated, placing this criterion firmly in the middle band.

strengths: Choice of RealClear Polling dataset shows personal motivation and thoughtful data justification
Rationale for starting data collection 11 weeks before the election reflects planning
weaknesses: Little evidence of creativity or initiative (no alternative models, error analysis or original data collection)
Mathematical approach remains standard and follows a template with minimal innovation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student demonstrates significant independent thinking in data selection and personal motivation (band 2), the lack of creativity or initiative in applying or extending the mathematics lowers the overall engagement to band 1.

strengths: Reflective note on regression limitations indicates some awareness of model boundaries
weaknesses: No in-depth consideration of assumptions, sampling bias or error sources
Superficial analysis of r values without critical discussion of reliability
Conclusion repeats predictions without evaluating implications or proposing improvements
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reflection is limited to brief comments on regression limitations and basic analysis of results (band 1). There is no substantial critical reflection, insight into error or future implications, so this criterion remains at the lowest performing band.

strengths: Use of Pearsonâs correlation coefficient and linear regression is relevant and applied appropriately to predict election outcomes
Mathematics (regression, correlation) is commensurate with SL course level
Compilation of polling averages provides a solid dataset for analysis
weaknesses: Regression equations are not displayed and substitution calculations for predictions are missing
Hand-drawn regression lines lead to approximate results and minor inaccuracies
Detailed statistical procedure (e.g., step-by-step regression derivation) is not fully outlined
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (relevance and level appropriateness) meet band 3 descriptors, while understanding and accuracy have minor omissions (band 2). Overall, the mathematics is used appropriately at the SL level without the comprehensiveness required for higher bands, placing it at band 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations generally follow the order: collect data â plot â calculate r â draw line â predict.  Nevertheless, key logical links (e.g., why a weak r invalidates linear prediction) are missing, and commentary is occasionally fragmented.  Hence the communication shows only âsomeâ logical structure and clarity.

The student correctly computes r values and qualitatively interprets them, but the hand-drawn regression lines are approximate, and the numeric regression equations are missing.  Minor inaccuracies in reading graphs persist.  This indicates âsome understanding with errors,â corresponding to band 2.

Tables and scatter plots are presented alongside brief verbal description, providing at least two forms of representation.  Yet the axes are unlabelled or hand-drawn, scales are uneven, and there is no regression-equation display.  This satisfies âsome useâ but is not effective, so band 2 is appropriate.

The exploration has an identifiable introduction, body and conclusion, and the reader can follow the main idea of using polling data to predict an election.  However, frequent digressions (e.g., personal anecdotes, teacher comments left in the draft) interrupt the flow, and several sections are missing or appear out of place (e.g., tables after commentary).  These weaknesses lower overall coherence to the âsome coherenceâ level rather than the higher bands.

Apart from compiling the polling table and hand-drawing graphs, there is little evidence of creativity or initiative; no alternative models, error analysis or original data collection are attempted.  This corresponds to âsome creativity,â band 1.

The student provides a superficial analysis of r values and a short statement on the closeness of predicted percentages.  No critical discussion of sampling bias, model fit, or confidence intervals is offered, so the work remains at the âbasic analysisâ level (band 1).

The conclusion repeats the numerical predictions but does not evaluate their reliability, nor does it propose improvements or future inquiry.  This constitutes a âlimited evaluation of outcomes,â therefore band 1.

Reflection is confined to brief comments that linear regression is âlimitedâ for fluctuating data.  There is no in-depth consideration of assumptions, sources of error, or alternative approaches.  This limited commentary aligns with band 1.

The student uses terms such as âcorrelation coefficient,â âr valueâ and âline of best fitâ correctly and includes numerical results, but variables are not defined and calculator syntax (âL1 Barâ) appears.  Notation is therefore only partially appropriate, consistent with band 2.

Personal motivation (Canadian observer interested in U.S. politics) is woven through the narrative, and the topic is clearly the studentâs own.  However, the mathematical approach is standard and not deeply personalised, placing it in the middle band.

Much of the text is relevant to linear regression and polling; however, redundancy (multiple explanations of why polls matter) and inclusion of teacherâs marginal notes add unnecessary length.  Therefore the work is mostly relevant yet contains some superfluous material, matching band 2.

Simple statistical toolsâcorrelation coefficient and linear regressionâare clearly relevant to analysing polling trends.  They are applied in context to generate predictions, satisfying the descriptor ârelevant mathematics used appropriatelyâ at band 3.  The work is not extensive enough for higher bands that require comprehensive or expert use.

Selecting her own dataset, justifying the choice of RealClear averages, and deciding to compare two candidates indicate significant but not outstanding independent thinking.  The work moves beyond a template yet does not include novel mathematical insight, so band 2 fits best.

Headings such as Introduction, Methodology, Data, Evaluation and Bibliography give the work a recognisable structure, but the table of contents is corrupt, figures are not numbered sequentially, and data tables/graphs are separated from the text that discusses them.  This represents âadequate organisation with basic structure,â but not the clear, consistently ordered presentation required for band 3 or above.

Linear regression and Pearsonâs r are part of the SL curriculum and are applied at that expected level.  The mathematics is neither below nor beyond course content, so band 3ââcommensurate with course levelââis the best match.

annotations.comment: The title is clear and descriptive, establishing the focus of the exploration effectively.
Choosing RealClear Polling shows significant independent thinking and appropriate data justification.
The comment on averaging different sources is useful but is teacher feedback; remove it and integrate relevant rationale in your own words.
The teacherâs note âUnorganized... include your data table here.â indicates incomplete structuring. Integrate your table adjacent to its discussion.
The table of contents appears corrupt and unreadable. Revise or rebuild it to reflect correct page numbering and sections.
The Aims section is left blank. Ensure you clearly state your aim and research questions to frame the investigation.
The placeholder âMethodology...................................â needs substantive content. Outline your data collection and analytical procedures here.
Bibliography is placed on page 1 but appears at the end; renumber and position consistently in the final document.
Remove the teacherâs note âTitle is not necessary.â and replace with your own refined introduction to maintain academic tone.
The introduction provides context but lacks an explicit statement of your aim. Include a clear research question to guide the reader.
The header âAim and ?? Methodologyâ is confusing. Use clear section headings and correct formatting to signal structure.
Methodology describes site selection but lacks detail on how linear regression will be applied. Outline step-by-step statistical procedures.
The rationale for starting data collection 11 weeks before the election is clearly explained, demonstrating thoughtful planning.
The header row âABC 123 123â in your data table is meaningless. Use descriptive labels for table headers (e.g., Date, Trump %, Harris %).
Axes and unit labels are missing from the table and accompanying figures. Label columns and axes clearly for reader comprehension.
The compilation of polling averages from multiple dates provides a solid dataset for regression analysis.
When describing the axes, explicitly define your variables (e.g., let $$x$$ = week number, $$y$$ = polling percentage).
The note âDefine your variables. B-â should be replaced by a formal definition of variables in your own text.
Figure 2 is a useful scatter plot but lacks the regression equation and line of best fit; include these to support later calculations.
Avoid calculator-specific notation like âL1 Bar.â Present correlation calculations abstractly, e.g., â$$r = 0.8053$$.â
Explain the interpretation of $$r$$ values: for example, $$r=0.8053$$ indicates strong positive linear correlation, while $$r=-0.1195$$ is weak.
Plotting the mean as the central point in Figures 3 and 4 shows thoughtful analysis of data distribution.
The caption âFigure 3: What % of the US is voting for Trumpâ¦â effectively clarifies the data scope and range.
Graphs lack function labels (e.g., regression equation). Label each fitted function $$y=mx+b$$ to communicate your model.
The description of fitting the line of best fit is clear, but you must supply the equation (e.g., $$y=0.15x+47.2$$).
Figure 4 omits the regression line equation. Display $$y=mx+c$$ and indicate the values of $$m$$ and $$c$$ on the graph.
You quote predicted percentages but do not show the calculation using your regression equations. Include the substitution of election day value into $$y=mx+b$$.
Remove the teacher note âFind the line of regression and use it.â and insert your formal regression derivation instead.
The conclusion summarises findings but does not critically evaluate the reliability or error of predictions; discuss confidence intervals or bias.
The reflective note on regression limitations demonstrates some critical thought on model applicability.
Bibliography entries lack consistent formatting and complete details (author, title, date). Follow a recognized citation style.
Remove the teacherâs personal feedback (âKhalisaâI enjoyed reading your IAâ¦â). This should not appear in the final draft.
The teacherâs rubric comments (e.g., âAâ The exploration has some coherenceâ¦â) are not part of your analysis and should be excluded.
